title: Initial Development of MBE-Grown InAs Diodes for Thermoradiative Energy Harvesting
authors: I. Artacho, I. Ramiro, A. Martí
abstract: We describe the development of 1x1 mm2 InAs thermoradiative diodes grown by molecular beam epitaxy with emphasis on their reverse saturation current and break-down voltage. P-i-n diode structures grown at 450 C, with As2 flux around 3 times stoichiometry and an In effusion cell tip temperature 150 C higher than the base temperature, exhibit the best results with breakdown voltages above 0.3 V and reverse saturation current densities 200 times the radiative limit.
summary: This paper details the experimental development of InAs-based diodes designed for thermoradiative energy harvesting. By optimizing MBE growth parameters and introducing a graded InGaAs layer, the researchers significantly improved the diode's breakdown voltage and reduced leakage current toward the theoretical radiative limit.
main_problem: Developing a reliable manufacturing route using Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) to create InAs thermoradiative diodes with low reverse saturation current and high breakdown voltage.
main_result: The implementation of a graded p+-In0.95Ga0.05As top layer and optimized growth parameters achieved a breakdown voltage above 0.3 V and reduced the reverse saturation current density to 200 times the radiative limit.
method: InAs p-i-n diode structures were grown via Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) on n-type substrates, with various structural modifications (B12-B15) tested and characterized using J-V measurements and RHEED.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of 1x1 mm2 InAs p-i-n diodes acting as thermal engines, operating in the thermoradiative (TR) regime where the diode is hotter than the ambient environment.
key_observables: Reverse saturation current density (J0), breakdown voltage, electron mobility, and bandgap (Eg).
important_parameters: Substrate temperature (450-500 C), As2 flux (3x stoichiometry), In effusion cell temperature gradient, and the Shockley and Queisser (S&Q) radiative limit.
main_assumptions: The performance is evaluated against the theoretical Shockley and Queisser detailed balance limit for radiative recombination.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows schematic diagrams of the four different InAs diode growth structures; Figure 3 displays J-V characteristics for each batch compared against the theoretical radiative limit; Table I summarizes growth parameters and resulting J0.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of thermoradiative energy harvesting, describes the MBE growth process and various diode architectures, presents the experimental characterization results, and concludes with the optimization of the graded layer.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Simulated current-voltage characteristic of an ideal radiative InAs pn diode operating: non-illuminated (black) with 𝑇𝑇𝑆𝑆= 𝑇𝑇𝐶𝐶= 300 K; in thermophotovoltaic operation (TPV) when illuminated by a black body at 𝑇𝑇𝑆𝑆=550 K under maximum concentrated light and the diode at 𝑇𝑇𝐶𝐶=300 K (red); in thermoradiative (TR) mode with the diode operated at 𝑇𝑇𝐶𝐶=550 K with the surroundings at 𝑇𝑇𝑆𝑆=300 K. Notice that the TPV and TR current densities plotted are the result of dividing the actual current density by a factor 100. In this radiative limit, the maximum power obtained in TPV mode is 81 W‧m‒2 and the maximum power in TR mode is 13 W‧m‒2. Voltages refer to the voltage at the p-side of the...

Fig. 2 sketches the four InAs diode structures manufactured by molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) on a Veeco Gen 10. Table I summarizes the MBE growth conditions. All the samples were grown on 2” InAs n+ (1,0,0) substrates, doped with S in the range 1‒3×1018 cm‒3 according to their manufacturer (Wafer Technology Ltd.)

Fig. 3. Best diode current density-voltage characteristic (defined by such that shows lower reverse saturation current density) for each batch. The reverse current-density limit for an InAs diode with back reflector is also shown for reference (dashed line). Measurements at 300 K. Diode area: 1×1 mm2. Comparison with the radiative limit refers to the case of emission towards a substrate for which case the limit is 𝐽𝐽01 ≈ 9.3×10‒4 A‧cm‒2.
